title: Git & GitHub
objectives: Collaborate through PRs with clean history
Use branches, rebase, cherry-pick and reflog fluently

sections.markdown: Git is a distributed version-control system tracking snapshots (commits) as an immutable graph. GitHub adds cloud hosting, pull requests, actions, and code review — the standard collaboration platform for most teams.

sections.code:
# Everyday flow
git switch -c feat/auth-refresh
# ...edit code, commit small, often
git commit -m "auth: refresh token before expiry"
git push -u origin feat/auth-refresh
# open PR on GitHub -> review -> merge (squash)

# Rescue tools
git reflog                 # every HEAD move you made
git cherry-pick <sha>      # pluck a single commit
git revert  <sha>          # undo a commit safely (no history rewrite)

rows: Require reviews / passing CI / signed commits

sections.markdown: Senior habits: **small commits with imperative subject lines**, one logical change per PR, squash-merge on default, protect main with 'require passing CI + 1 approval'. Rebase your feature branch to keep the graph linear; never force-push to shared branches.

Force-pushing to `main` (or any shared branch) — rewrites teammates' history and can lose work. If you must fix a public branch, use `git revert`. Reserve `push --force-with-lease` for your own feature branches.
Configure the repo: branch protection, CODEOWNERS, PR templates, required CI status checks. Use `gh` CLI for scripted operations. Store secrets in GitHub Actions env/environment, never in git.

flashcards.front: How do you recover a lost commit?
flashcards.back: `git reflog` → find the SHA → `git checkout` or `git reset --hard <sha>`.
revert creates a new commit undoing changes (safe on shared history); reset moves HEAD (rewrites history).
Use `--force-with-lease` on your own feature branches; never on shared/protected ones.

quiz.q: Best way to undo a bad commit on `main` after merge?
quiz.explain: Revert is safe for shared history.

quiz.q: Lost commits are typically recoverable via…

challenges.text: Open a PR from a feature branch, request review, squash-merge.
Rewrite messy commits with interactive rebase before opening the PR.
Recover a lost branch via reflog after a bad `reset --hard`.
Configure branch protection + CODEOWNERS + a required CI matrix for the repo.

interviewQuestions: How do you recover lost work?
Rebase vs merge for feature branches?
How do you keep a clean PR history?
